serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Additional Ayam is approximately 100g, typically served as a topping or side portion over rice or noodles in a Singaporean hawker or canteen setting.

nutrient_source.title: Additional Ayam — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Additional Ayam' (cr_id: G00010415) with confidence 0.95. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on sweet chicken dish with sugar-based sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for 'Additional Ayam' in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate profile: 34.39g/100g carbohydrate with 28.82g/100g sugar, consistent with sweet sauce-based chicken dishes. The high sugar fraction (predominantly sucrose from marinade/sauce) drives a medium-to-high GI response. Analogous reference: sweet and sour chicken-type dishes with sugar-based sauces, GI ~60–70 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed sweet meat dishes. GL per 100g serving = (65 × 34.39) / 100 = 22.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (28.82g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.5g/100g) triggers insulin-mediated sodium retention and promotes weight gain, independently elevating blood pressure risk despite relatively low sodium (102mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its very high sugar content (28.82g/100g), which drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and cardiovascular strain; if consumed, portion size should not exceed 50g and should be paired with high-fibre foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 28.82g/100g far exceeds the 20g/100g glycaemic ceiling for diabetes management, with a medium-high GI of 65 and glycaemic load of 22.4 per 100g serving, posing significant postprandial blood glucose spike risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to critically high sugar content (28.82g/100g) and high glycaemic load (22.4 per 100g); it should be avoided or consumed only in very small amounts (≤30g) with close blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of approximately 6.5g/100g from coconut milk exceeds the 5g/100g threshold for cardiovascular concern, combined with high sugar (28.82g/100g) which promotes dyslipidaemia, triglyceride elevation, and endothelial inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to the combination of high saturated fat (~6.5g/100g from coconut milk) and very high sugar (28.82g/100g), both of which independently and synergistically elevate LDL cholesterol, triglycerides, and systemic inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 28.82g/100g far exceeds the 15g/100g refined sugar threshold for RA, strongly promoting inflammatory mediators; saturated fat (~6.5g/100g) also exceeds the 5g/100g threshold, triggering TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to its very high sugar (28.82g/100g) and elevated saturated fat (~6.5g/100g), both of which are potent pro-inflammatory drivers; chilli content may also exacerbate symptoms in nightshade-sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Additional Ayam is a Singaporean chicken topping dish, typically served as an extra portion of cooked chicken over rice or noodles in hawker centres, food courts, and school canteens across Singapore. The dish features chicken pieces prepared with a sweet, spiced sauce or marinade that commonly includes sugar, coconut milk, chilli, and aromatic spices, resulting in a rich, sweet-savoury flavour profile with a tender, sauced texture. Per 100g, it provides 249 kcal, 3.93g protein, 11.76g fat, and 34.39g carbohydrate, of which 28.82g is sugar — an exceptionally high sugar fraction that dominates the nutritional profile. Dietary fibre is low at 1.5g/100g and sodium is moderate at 102mg/100g. The high sugar content (28.82g/100g) makes this dish clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The estimated saturated fat of approximately 6.5g/100g from coconut milk further elevates cardiovascular and inflammatory risk. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) with a glycaemic load of 22.4 per 100g, reflecting the high sugar-driven carbohydrate load. This dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner as a protein-and-sauce topping, though its nutritional profile is more characteristic of a sweet sauce dish than a lean protein source.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for 'Additional Ayam' (cr_id: G00010415) with a stated confidence of 0.95. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. Saturated fat is estimated from the known coconut milk composition of the dish as the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (6.5g/100g) is estimated based on the known coconut milk content of this dish type, as the HPB source record does not separately report saturated fat. This estimate is consistent with the health_suitability data provided in the input record. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean chicken topping preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010415 as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on sweet sauce-based chicken dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 4. The dish name 'Additional Ayam' reflects its function as an add-on chicken topping in Singaporean food service contexts; 'Ayam' is the Malay word for chicken. 5. All core macros (protein_g: 3.93, fat_g: 11.76, carbohydrate_g: 34.39) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
